title: Run a WordPress MCP Server in Cursor With Curated Abilities

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: I kept hitting the same wall with WordPress MCP tutorials: “connect Claude to your site in five minutes.” That line gets you a working demo. It does not get you a production workflow you would trust on a live blog—and when you are the only person running the site, that gap matters.

blocks.innerHTML: I kept hitting the same wall with WordPress MCP tutorials: “connect Claude to your site in five minutes.” That line gets you a working demo. It does not get you a production workflow you would trust on a live blog—and when you are the only person running the site, that gap matters.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: I run rootsandfruit.com as a one-person shop: self-hosted WordPress on Cloudways, Kadence, Breeze cache. I connect Cursor through one MCP server. I call curated rootsandfruit/* abilities instead of opening the whole REST surface to an agent. And I do not publish until I have read a public preview myself.

blocks.innerHTML: I run rootsandfruit.com as a one-person shop: self-hosted WordPress on Cloudways, Kadence, Breeze cache. I connect Cursor through one MCP server. I call curated rootsandfruit/* abilities instead of opening the whole REST surface to an agent. And I do not publish until I have read a public preview myself.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: So in this article I am going to walk you through the stack I actually use—not a theoretical setup. Take what fits your site. Leave what does not.

blocks.innerHTML: So in this article I am going to walk you through the stack I actually use—not a theoretical setup. Take what fits your site. Leave what does not.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Why generic MCP tutorials left me wanting more

blocks.innerHTML: Why generic MCP tutorials left me wanting more

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: When I searched “wordpress mcp server,” most results fell into three buckets: MCP explainers, links to Automattic’s wordpress-mcp repo, and listicles of MCP servers. All of that is useful for a first connection. Once you are already publishing, though, it gets thin—because almost none of it talks about what an agent should not be able to do.

blocks.innerHTML: When I searched “wordpress mcp server,” most results fell into three buckets: MCP explainers, links to Automattic’s wordpress-mcp repo, and listicles of MCP servers. All of that is useful for a first connection. Once you are already publishing, though, it gets thin—because almost none of it talks about what an agent should not be able to do.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: That is the part I had to learn the hard way. Here are the four gaps that kept showing up for me:

blocks.innerHTML: That is the part I had to learn the hard way. Here are the four gaps that kept showing up for me:

blocks.name: core/list

values: Least privilege. Broad MCP plugins expose a lot of tools. I wanted agents to help with content—not wander the whole install.
Block editor reality. Dumping HTML into post_content via REST breaks Gutenberg structure. Body edits need block-aware tools.
Publish gates. A preview URL is not the live URL. I needed an explicit approve step, plus a plan for byline and cache.
Self-hosted vs hosted. WordPress.com MCP docs do not map cleanly to my Cloudways stack. Follow the wrong guide and you lose an afternoon.

blocks.innerHTML:
• Least privilege. Broad MCP plugins expose a lot of tools. I wanted agents to help with content—not wander the whole install.
  • Block editor reality. Dumping HTML into post_content via REST breaks Gutenberg structure. Body edits need block-aware tools.
  • Publish gates. A preview URL is not the live URL. I needed an explicit approve step, plus a plan for byline and cache.
  • Self-hosted vs hosted. WordPress.com MCP docs do not map cleanly to my Cloudways stack. Follow the wrong guide and you lose an afternoon.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Once I named those gaps, the design choice got clearer. I stopped treating MCP as a magic pipe to “the whole site.” I started building a narrow, reviewed surface instead—and that decision shapes everything below.

blocks.innerHTML: Once I named those gaps, the design choice got clearer. I stopped treating MCP as a magic pipe to “the whole site.” I started building a narrow, reviewed surface instead—and that decision shapes everything below.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: MCP, Abilities API, and MCP Adapter (plain language)

blocks.innerHTML: MCP, Abilities API, and MCP Adapter (plain language)

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Before we get into my architecture, it helps to align on three terms. They show up in every official doc, and I found it easier to move fast once I had a plain-language read on each one.

blocks.innerHTML: Before we get into my architecture, it helps to align on three terms. They show up in every official doc, and I found it easier to move fast once I had a plain-language read on each one.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is how Cursor (or Claude Desktop) talks to external tools. On WordPress, those tools are things like “create a draft,” “insert a paragraph block,” or “enable a public preview link.” Think of MCP as the wire between your editor and your site.

blocks.innerHTML: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is how Cursor (or Claude Desktop) talks to external tools. On WordPress, those tools are things like “create a draft,” “insert a paragraph block,” or “enable a public preview link.” Think of MCP as the wire between your editor and your site.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: WordPress 6.9+ adds the Abilities API. That is where you register named abilities in PHP—with schemas, permission callbacks, and execute handlers. Your site decides what exists. A generic client does not get to guess.

blocks.innerHTML: WordPress 6.9+ adds the Abilities API. That is where you register named abilities in PHP—with schemas, permission callbacks, and execute handlers. Your site decides what exists. A generic client does not get to guess.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: From there, the MCP Adapter (announcement) exposes those registered abilities to MCP clients. Cursor sees them as tools. WordPress still enforces capabilities on every call. That last point is important: MCP does not bypass WordPress permissions, and I would not want it to.

blocks.innerHTML: From there, the MCP Adapter (announcement) exposes those registered abilities to MCP clients. Cursor sees them as tools. WordPress still enforces capabilities on every call. That last point is important: MCP does not bypass WordPress permissions, and I would not want it to.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: On my site, the chain looks like this:

blocks.innerHTML: On my site, the chain looks like this:

blocks.name: core/list

values: My abilities plugin (Roots-and-Fruit/abilities) registers rootsandfruit/* abilities.
MCP Adapter (separate plugin) bridges them to MCP.
Cursor connects with one entry in .cursor/mcp.json, authenticated via Application Password.

blocks.innerHTML:
1. My abilities plugin (Roots-and-Fruit/abilities) registers rootsandfruit/* abilities.
  2. MCP Adapter (separate plugin) bridges them to MCP.
  3. Cursor connects with one entry in .cursor/mcp.json, authenticated via Application Password.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: You still need WordPress 6.9+, both plugins active, and a user with the right capabilities. With that foundation in place, the next question is how many MCP servers you actually need—which is where a lot of setups get messy.

blocks.innerHTML: You still need WordPress 6.9+, both plugins active, and a user with the right capabilities. With that foundation in place, the next question is how many MCP servers you actually need—which is where a lot of setups get messy.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Architecture: one Cursor MCP to rootsandfruit.com

blocks.innerHTML: Architecture: one Cursor MCP to rootsandfruit.com

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Early on I asked myself a simple question: how many MCP servers should Cursor talk to for one WordPress site? My answer today is one: wordpress-rootsandfruit. No second Node process for blocks.

blocks.innerHTML: Early on I asked myself a simple question: how many MCP servers should Cursor talk to for one WordPress site? My answer today is one: wordpress-rootsandfruit. No second Node process for blocks.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Here is the flow I settled on:

blocks.innerHTML: Here is the flow I settled on:

blocks.name: core/code

attributes.content:
Cursor IDE
    │
    ▼  MCP (Application Password)
wordpress-rootsandfruit
    │
    ▼
MCP Adapter plugin
    │
    ▼
rootsandfruit-abilities plugin  ──►  rootsandfruit/* abilities
    │
    └──►  GK Block MCP (in-process PHP)  ──►  blocks-* abilities

blocks.innerHTML:
Cursor IDE
    │
    ▼  MCP (Application Password)
wordpress-rootsandfruit
    │
    ▼
MCP Adapter plugin
    │
    ▼
rootsandfruit-abilities plugin  ──►  rootsandfruit/* abilities
    │
    └──►  GK Block MCP (in-process PHP)  ──►  blocks-* abilities

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: I did consider adding @gravitykit/block-mcp as a second Cursor MCP. In the end I skipped it. Block operations already run in-process through my plugin’s bridge to GravityKit Block MCP. A second server would duplicate config, split routing (“REST here, Node MCP there”), and make agent instructions harder to keep safe.

blocks.innerHTML: I did consider adding @gravitykit/block-mcp as a second Cursor MCP. In the end I skipped it. Block operations already run in-process through my plugin’s bridge to GravityKit Block MCP. A second server would duplicate config, split routing (“REST here, Node MCP there”), and make agent instructions harder to keep safe.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: So I kept one server, one routing doc, and one workflow I can explain in a single blog post. From there I split responsibilities on purpose: block work goes through rootsandfruit/blocks-*; title and excerpt go through rootsandfruit/update-post. I enforce that split in my agent rules.

blocks.innerHTML: So I kept one server, one routing doc, and one workflow I can explain in a single blog post. From there I split responsibilities on purpose: block work goes through rootsandfruit/blocks-*; title and excerpt go through rootsandfruit/update-post. I enforce that split in my agent rules.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: That routing split only makes sense once you know which abilities exist—which brings us to the curated list I actually expose.

blocks.innerHTML: That routing split only makes sense once you know which abilities exist—which brings us to the curated list I actually expose.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Curated rootsandfruit/* abilities (what I expose-and what I don't)

blocks.innerHTML: Curated rootsandfruit/* abilities (what I expose—and what I don’t)

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Generic MCP setups often mirror large parts of the REST API. I took the opposite approach: a small, reviewed set of abilities with explicit permission checks on each one.

blocks.innerHTML: Generic MCP setups often mirror large parts of the REST API. I took the opposite approach: a small, reviewed set of abilities with explicit permission checks on each one.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: The live list grows as I ship plugin releases. After every deploy I run audit-mcp-abilities.ps1 or MCP discover to confirm what Cursor actually sees—because the doc you write and the tools Cursor discovers should match.

blocks.innerHTML: The live list grows as I ship plugin releases. After every deploy I run audit-mcp-abilities.ps1 or MCP discover to confirm what Cursor actually sees—because the doc you write and the tools Cursor discovers should match.

blocks.name: core/table

head: Module
Abilities
Permission basis
How I use them
body: Smoke test; confirms plugin version and block bridge
list-posts, get-post, create-draft, update-post, publish-post, set-post-author
blocks-get-page, blocks-update, blocks-mutate, blocks-insert, blocks-create-page, blocks-list-patterns

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: The table shows what I do expose. The omissions matter just as much.

blocks.innerHTML: The table shows what I do expose. The omissions matter just as much.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: What I deliberately left out: post delete, trash, cache purge. Agents cannot remove content through my MCP surface. Breeze cache clears stay manual—I purge after byline changes so logged-out preview matches what I expect.

blocks.innerHTML: What I deliberately left out: post delete, trash, cache purge. Agents cannot remove content through my MCP surface. Breeze cache clears stay manual—I purge after byline changes so logged-out preview matches what I expect.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: REST escape hatches still exist for gaps I have not codified in the plugin yet. For day-to-day content I stick to rootsandfruit/*—especially blocks-* for body and set-post-author for bylines (v1.5.1+).

blocks.innerHTML: REST escape hatches still exist for gaps I have not codified in the plugin yet. For day-to-day content I stick to rootsandfruit/*—especially blocks-* for body and set-post-author for bylines (v1.5.1+).

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: When an agent call comes in, I route it with a simple rule of thumb: block markup → blocks-*; title, excerpt, or status → update-post / publish-post; byline → set-post-author. That rule saves me from the most common mistake in this stack—which is trying to push body HTML through the wrong ability.

blocks.innerHTML: When an agent call comes in, I route it with a simple rule of thumb: block markup → blocks-*; title, excerpt, or status → update-post / publish-post; byline → set-post-author. That rule saves me from the most common mistake in this stack—which is trying to push body HTML through the wrong ability.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Self-hosted vs WordPress.com MCP

blocks.innerHTML: Self-hosted vs WordPress.com MCP

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Even with the right abilities registered, I still see people follow the wrong setup guide—and that usually traces back to hosted vs self-hosted confusion.

blocks.innerHTML: Even with the right abilities registered, I still see people follow the wrong setup guide—and that usually traces back to hosted vs self-hosted confusion.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: WordPress.com documents MCP in a hosted context. My site is self-hosted: my plugins, my Application Passwords, my Cloudways server, my cache layer. Same vocabulary on the page, different moving parts behind it.

blocks.innerHTML: WordPress.com documents MCP in a hosted context. My site is self-hosted: my plugins, my Application Passwords, my Cloudways server, my cache layer. Same vocabulary on the page, different moving parts behind it.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: If you are on Cloudways, Kadence, or a similar stack, follow Abilities API + MCP Adapter + your own abilities plugin—not a WordPress.com-only setup guide. The mental model is the same (MCP tools → WordPress). The install paths, credentials, and cache behavior are not.

blocks.innerHTML: If you are on Cloudways, Kadence, or a similar stack, follow Abilities API + MCP Adapter + your own abilities plugin—not a WordPress.com-only setup guide. The mental model is the same (MCP tools → WordPress). The install paths, credentials, and cache behavior are not.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: I see the mismatch in forums all the time: “I connected MCP but my blocks are wrong” or “preview works logged-in only.” In most cases it is the wrong doc path, a missing block bridge, or skipping public preview entirely. Once you are on the right path for self-hosted, the next friction point is usually how body content gets written—which is where Gutenberg rules kick in.

blocks.innerHTML: I see the mismatch in forums all the time: “I connected MCP but my blocks are wrong” or “preview works logged-in only.” In most cases it is the wrong doc path, a missing block bridge, or skipping public preview entirely. Once you are on the right path for self-hosted, the next friction point is usually how body content gets written—which is where Gutenberg rules kick in.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Block body rules: innerHTML, not HTML via update-post

blocks.innerHTML: Block body rules: innerHTML, not HTML via update-post

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Gutenberg stores posts as block comments and structured JSON—not a single HTML blob. That is why rootsandfruit/update-post is for title, excerpt, and status—not body HTML. If you try to shove the article body through update-post, you are fighting the editor instead of working with it.

blocks.innerHTML: Gutenberg stores posts as block comments and structured JSON—not a single HTML blob. That is why rootsandfruit/update-post is for title, excerpt, and status—not body HTML. If you try to shove the article body through update-post, you are fighting the editor instead of working with it.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Body work on my site flows through block abilities instead:

blocks.innerHTML: Body work on my site flows through block abilities instead:

blocks.name: core/list

values: blocks-create-page — draft shell when I am starting from markdown.
blocks-get-page — read the block tree before I mutate anything.
blocks-insert — add blocks; for static blocks like core/heading and core/paragraph, include innerHTML or you get empty paragraphs on save.
blocks-mutate — targeted edits when I know block IDs.

blocks.innerHTML:
• blocks-create-page — draft shell when I am starting from markdown.
  • blocks-get-page — read the block tree before I mutate anything.
  • blocks-insert — add blocks; for static blocks like core/heading and core/paragraph, include innerHTML or you get empty paragraphs on save.
  • blocks-mutate — targeted edits when I know block IDs.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Blank paragraphs after an agent run? Nine times out of ten someone skipped innerHTML. That is why I verify with blocks-get-page after every insert—it is a quick sanity check before I move on to preview.

blocks.innerHTML: Blank paragraphs after an agent run? Nine times out of ten someone skipped innerHTML. That is why I verify with blocks-get-page after every insert—it is a quick sanity check before I move on to preview.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Getting blocks right is only half the publish story, though. The other half is the human gate between draft and live.

blocks.innerHTML: Getting blocks right is only half the publish story, though. The other half is the human gate between draft and live.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Draft → author → public preview → publish gate

blocks.innerHTML: Draft → author → public preview → publish gate

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Here is how I think about the handoff: agents edit drafts; I approve publish; preview is the checkpoint between those two.

blocks.innerHTML: Here is how I think about the handoff: agents edit drafts; I approve publish; preview is the checkpoint between those two.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: This is the checklist I run on rootsandfruit.com before anything goes live:

blocks.innerHTML: This is the checklist I run on rootsandfruit.com before anything goes live:

blocks.name: core/list

values: blocks-create-page (or create-draft + block insert) — draft shell with block body.
blocks-get-page — verify structure; confirm headings and paragraphs in block JSON.
set-post-author — assign the WordPress user that should appear on the byline.
Purge Breeze cache — manual, after byline change, so logged-out preview is trustworthy.
enable-public-preview — tokenized URL I can share without handing out wp-admin.
STOP — I read the preview URL; voice, links, and information-gain commitments have to clear the bar.
publish-post — only when I explicitly say go.

blocks.innerHTML:
1. blocks-create-page (or create-draft + block insert) — draft shell with block body.
  2. blocks-get-page — verify structure; confirm headings and paragraphs in block JSON.
  3. set-post-author — assign the WordPress user that should appear on the byline.
  4. Purge Breeze cache — manual, after byline change, so logged-out preview is trustworthy.
  5. enable-public-preview — tokenized URL I can share without handing out wp-admin.
  6. STOP — I read the preview URL; voice, links, and information-gain commitments have to clear the bar.
  7. publish-post — only when I explicitly say go.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Step 6 is the one I treat as non-negotiable. A public preview URL is not the live permalink. I treat preview as the last read-through, not shipping.

blocks.innerHTML: Step 6 is the one I treat as non-negotiable. A public preview URL is not the live permalink. I treat preview as the last read-through, not shipping.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Before I run any of that, I start with ping and expect block_mcp_active: true when the block bridge is live. If that fails, I fix the bridge first—there is no point building a draft on a broken path.

blocks.innerHTML: Before I run any of that, I start with ping and expect block_mcp_active: true when the block bridge is live. If that fails, I fix the bridge first—there is no point building a draft on a broken path.

blocks.name: core/heading

attributes.content: Verify your setup, then ship a draft

blocks.innerHTML: Verify your setup, then ship a draft

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: If you wire Cursor MCP the way I describe here, I would start with proof—not faith. Here is the sequence I use:

blocks.innerHTML: If you wire Cursor MCP the way I describe here, I would start with proof—not faith. Here is the sequence I use:

blocks.name: core/list

values: Run rootsandfruit/ping via MCP and confirm plugin version (1.5.1+ if you need set-post-author).
From your agent repo, run smoke scripts against production discover:

blocks.innerHTML:
1. Run rootsandfruit/ping via MCP and confirm plugin version (1.5.1+ if you need set-post-author).
  2. From your agent repo, run smoke scripts against production discover:

blocks.name: core/code

attributes.content:
.\tools\scripts\test-wordpress-mcp-http.ps1 -ExpectRfAbilities -ExpectBlocks
   .\tools\scripts\audit-mcp-abilities.ps1 -ExpectBlocks

blocks.innerHTML:
.\tools\scripts\test-wordpress-mcp-http.ps1 -ExpectRfAbilities -ExpectBlocks
   .\tools\scripts\audit-mcp-abilities.ps1 -ExpectBlocks

blocks.name: core/list

values: Ship your first piece as draft + preview, not straight to publish.

blocks.innerHTML: 1. Ship your first piece as draft + preview, not straight to publish.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: Plugin source and ability registrations: github.com/Roots-and-Fruit/abilities. Official references: MCP Adapter, Abilities API.

blocks.innerHTML: Plugin source and ability registrations: github.com/Roots-and-Fruit/abilities. Official references: MCP Adapter, Abilities API.

blocks.name: core/paragraph

attributes.content: If you are building a similar stack, start narrow—one MCP server, a short ability list, preview before publish. That is how I keep a solo operation moving without pretending I have a platform team behind me. Get the first draft live in preview, read it like a reader would, and iterate from there.

blocks.innerHTML: If you are building a similar stack, start narrow—one MCP server, a short ability list, preview before publish. That is how I keep a solo operation moving without pretending I have a platform team behind me. Get the first draft live in preview, read it like a reader would, and iterate from there.
